data.title: Many scaled rivals plus import price floor push Year-1 share to floor
data.narrative: The model assumes the entrant can win 0.03 of its obtainable share in Year 1, drawn from a single 15.8% Schneider datapoint. But the evidence shows five to seven co-equal scaled vendors and 60–70% import dependence in Eastern Europe setting a low price floor. That structure means more players able to price-match any pilot win, and the model prices their response at zero. This pins Year-1 obtainable share at the 0.01 floor, not the 0.03 mid.

mechanism.text: First bids meet 5–7 scaled incumbents and a low import price floor
Incumbents discount and bundle inside accounts; model sets response to zero
Lower win-rates cut Year-1 share to 0.01 floor, not 0.03 mid

data.whyMissable: 'Medium concentration' reads as a friendly middle, but fragmentation among scaled incumbents means more rivals able to price-match any niche the entrant opens.
data.falsifier: Entrant win-rate at least 30% across the first 10 contested bids, with no material price concession beyond plan.
data.likelihoodRationale: Incumbent in-account price defense is near-universal for new entrants, and the corpus documents the multi-incumbent structure and import price floor. The offset is that intelligent-PDU differentiation sometimes escapes pure price competition, so the leap to the 0.01 floor stays partly inferential.
